# Why?

## The Problem

**Cybersecurity incidents are growing in frequency and complexity.**

- Security teams must communicate threat patterns to management quickly
- Raw incident tables are hard to scan and even harder to present
- Comparing attack frequency versus attack severity requires manual aggregation
- Patterns across country, industry, attack type, and vulnerability are hidden in tabular data

. . .

**Today's reality:** Analysts export CSVs, build static charts in Excel, and assemble slide decks manually — for every reporting cycle.

## What We Built

An **interactive web-based visual report** that makes patterns in 3,000 cybersecurity incidents immediately visible:

- Trend overview: how incidents evolved from 2015 to 2024
- Country and industry comparisons by frequency *and* severity
- Attack type and vulnerability profiles
- Defense mechanism outcomes

. . .

All in a **single Quarto site** — no manual aggregation, published on GitHub Pages.


# For Whom?

## Persona 1 — Sophia, Senior Cybersecurity Analyst {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- Age 34, works in a security operations environment
- Prepares internal reports and trend analysis regularly
- **High** domain expertise and data literacy
- Uses a 27" desktop monitor
- Needs analytical depth and cross-category comparison

> *"I need to see which vulnerability types are exploited most in Banking — and whether that's different from Healthcare."*
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Jobs:**

- Analyse attack trends by year, country, industry
- Compare vulnerabilities and defense outcomes
- Prepare evidence-based briefings

**Pains:**

- Manually combining static charts is time-consuming
- Existing summaries don't support multi-dimensional comparison
- Hard to distinguish frequent threats from severe ones
:::
::::

## Persona 2 — Marcus, IT Security Manager {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- Age 48, responsible for security priorities at management level
- Communicates risk to senior leadership
- **Medium** data literacy, limited time for exploration
- Uses a 14" corporate laptop
- Needs a clear entry point and fast time-to-insight

> *"Show me the most affected industries and the biggest financial losses — I need to justify our budget priorities."*
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Jobs:**

- Get a quick overview of key threat patterns
- Identify high-risk sectors and attack types
- Communicate findings to executive stakeholders

**Pains:**

- Complex dashboards create friction and reduce trust
- Purely technical views don't translate to management communication
- Hard to distinguish frequency from severity at a glance
:::
::::


# Data Source

## Kaggle — Global Cybersecurity Threats (2015–2024)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**What is it?**

A public dataset of 3,000 cybersecurity incidents spanning 2015 to 2024, published on Kaggle (CC0 licence).

**What does it cover?**

- 10 countries: Australia, Brazil, China, France, Germany, India, Japan, Russia, UK, USA
- 7 industries: Banking, Education, Government, Healthcare, IT, Retail, Telecommunications
- 10 variables per incident
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Variable overview:**

| Variable | Type |
|---|---|
| Country, Year | Context |
| Attack Type, Attack Source | Threat |
| Target Industry, Vulnerability | Target |
| Defense Mechanism | Response |
| Financial Loss (M$) | Severity |
| Affected Users | Severity |
| Resolution Time (h) | Severity |

:::
::::

## Data Acquisition

**Automated download via `data_acquisition/load.py`:**

```python
import kaggle
kaggle.api.dataset_download_files(
    "atharvasoundankar/global-cybersecurity-threats-2015-2024",
    path="data/raw/", unzip=True
)
```

- Credentials stored in `.env` (never committed)
- Any team member can reproduce the acquisition independently
- Column names normalised to lowercase snake_case at load time
- No missing values — no imputation required


# Exploratory Data Analysis

## Dataset Overview

::: {#cell-fig-eda-overview .cell execution_count=2}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Incident counts by country and industry.](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/fig-eda-overview-output-1.png){#fig-eda-overview width=1046 height=374}
:::
:::


All categories are **evenly distributed** — no single country or industry dominates the raw counts.

## Severity Dimensions Are Independent

::: {#cell-fig-eda-corr .cell execution_count=3}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Pearson correlations between the three numerical severity measures.](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/fig-eda-corr-output-1.png){#fig-eda-corr width=420 height=373}
:::
:::


Near-zero correlations confirm that **financial loss, affected users, and resolution time measure distinct dimensions** of severity — all three should be surfaced in the visualisation.

## Attack Types and Vulnerability Distribution

::: {#cell-fig-eda-attack .cell execution_count=4}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Distribution of attack types and vulnerability types.](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/fig-eda-attack-output-1.png){#fig-eda-attack width=1046 height=374}
:::
:::


# The Visualisation Product

## Structure — From Overview to Detail

The report is divided into **five sections**, supporting both quick orientation and deeper exploration:

| Section | Content | Primary user |
|---|---|---|
| **Overview** | KPI cards, headline totals | Marcus (management) |
| **Trends** | Incident count and severity over 2015–2024 | Both |
| **Countries & Industries** | Frequency and severity comparisons | Both |
| **Threat Profiles** | Attack types, vulnerabilities, attack sources | Sophia (analyst) |
| **Defense & Outcomes** | Defense mechanisms vs. resolution time and loss | Sophia (analyst) |

## Overview Section — Immediate Orientation

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**KPI cards** at the top of the page:

- Total incidents recorded
- Average financial loss (M$)
- Average resolution time (h)
- Most frequent attack type

**Year trend sparkline** below the cards for immediate temporal context

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Why?**

- Addresses Marcus's need for a **fast entry point**
- Answers "how big is the problem overall?" before any filtering
- KPI cards are a familiar management communication format
- Sets scale before comparative charts appear
:::
::::

## Trends Section — Temporal Patterns

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Line charts** across 2015–2024 for:

- Incident count per year
- Average financial loss per year
- Average affected users per year
- Average resolution time per year

**Metric toggle** to switch between severity dimensions
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Why line charts?**

- Time series data maps naturally to lines
- Allows users to see whether threats are growing, stable, or declining
- Metric toggle avoids chart overload while keeping all severity dimensions accessible
- Directly answers the first research question from the project charta
:::
::::

## Countries & Industries — Comparative Views

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Sorted bar charts** for country and industry comparisons:

- Switchable metric: count / financial loss / affected users / resolution time
- Sorted by selected metric so ranking is immediately visible

**Grouped bar chart** for side-by-side severity comparison across countries
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Why sorted bars instead of a map?**

- Dataset contains only 10 countries — precise comparison matters more than geography
- Sorted bars make ranking unambiguous
- A choropleth would waste space and add visual complexity without analytical gain
- Directly addresses Marcus's question: *"Which industries face the greatest losses?"*
:::
::::

## Threat Profiles — Attack and Vulnerability Analysis

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Heatmaps** for cross-category concentration:

- Attack type × industry
- Vulnerability type × industry
- Attack source × country

**Sorted bar charts** for attack type frequency and severity rankings
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Why heatmaps here?**

- Heatmaps efficiently show concentration patterns across two categorical dimensions simultaneously
- Enables Sophia to answer: *"Which vulnerability types are most common in Banking?"*
- Colour intensity encodes frequency or severity without requiring separate charts per combination
:::
::::

## Defense & Outcomes — Do Defenses Make a Difference?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Box plots** comparing:

- Financial loss distribution by defense mechanism
- Resolution time distribution by defense mechanism
- Affected users distribution by defense mechanism

Sorted by median value for easy ranking
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Why box plots?**

- Distributions reveal spread and outliers — not just averages
- Avoids misleading summary statistics when underlying distributions are wide
- Enables Sophia to assess whether mechanisms like AI-based Detection or MFA are genuinely associated with better outcomes
- Supports evidence-based recommendations without causal overclaiming
:::
::::

## Design Decisions Summary {.smaller}

| Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Sorted bar charts** over choropleth maps | Only 10 countries — precise ranking more useful than geography |
| **Metric toggle** (count / loss / users / time) | Avoids chart overload; keeps frequency vs. severity distinction explicit |
| **Box plots** for defense outcomes | Distributions are more honest than means for skewed severity data |
| **Heatmaps** for cross-category analysis | Compact representation of two-dimensional categorical relationships |
| **Section structure** (overview → detail) | Supports both fast management orientation and deeper analyst exploration |
| **No causal claims** in annotations | Dataset is synthetic and balanced — findings are described as patterns within the data |
| **GitHub Pages deployment** | Reproducible, version-controlled, accessible without installation |


# Thank You {.center}

**Global Cybersecurity Threats 2015–2024**

Vithujan Shanmugaratnam · Francis Lehner · Brian Bernhauser

ZHAW — VDSS Project 2026
